title: 2 4 Level surfaces
body:
2.4 LEVEL SURFACES, TANGENT PLANES & NORMAL LINES

     The level surface of a scalar field Žå(x, y, z) is a surface formed by all the points
    (x, y, z) with the same value of Žå. In two dimensions this is called the level curve.


    The level surface corresponding to the value c of the scalar field is described by the
     equation Žå(x, y, z) = c. At every point on the level surface, the gradient of Žå is
                           perpendicular to the level surface.


   The tangent plane to the surface Žå(x, y, z) = c at the point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) is given by

                       ŌłćŽå|P ┬Ę [(x ŌłÆ x0 ) i + (y ŌłÆ y0 ) j + (z ŌłÆ z0 ) k] = 0.



    The normal line to the surface Žå(x, y, z) = c at the point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) is given by

                                      r(t) = r0 + ŌłćŽå|P t

  where r(t) = x(t)i + y(t)j + z(t)k, r0 = x0 i + y0 j + z0 k and t is the distance along the
                         normal line measured from the point P .


 Example 1 Consider the temperature field T (x, y, z) = x2 + y 2 + z 2 . Find the temper-
 ature at the point P (3, 0, 4) and describe the surface of all points whose temperature is
 the same as that of P (This is called a level surface).

 In the previous two examples we have seen that the gradient ŌłćŽå at a point P is perpen-
dicular to the level surface (or curve in 2-D) at that point. In fact, this is always the case
ŌĆö at every point on a level surface Žå(x, y, z) = c, the gradient of Žå is perpendicular to
the level surface. We will prove this fascinating little result the next Topic.

The gradient can also be used to define the tangent plane and normal line to a surface.
Let us first recall how to define planes in three dimensions.

An Intuitive Verification of the Method of Lagrange (Optional)

Recall that the method of Lagrange Multipliers states that to find the local minima and
maxima of f (x, y) subject to the constraint g(x, y) = 0 we find the values of x, y and ╬╗
that simultaneously satisfy the equations
             Ōłéf    Ōłég           Ōłéf    Ōłég
                ŌłÆ╬╗    = 0,         ŌłÆ╬╗    = 0,      together with g(x, y) = 0.
             Ōłéx    Ōłéx           Ōłéy    Ōłéy


We can now justify this quite easily:

The constraint g(x, y) = 0 is simply a level curve for the scalar field g(x, y). Pick a point
P on the curve g(x, y) = 0. At P , grad(g) will point in the direction of maximal increase
of g(x, y) and will also point perpendicular to the level curve g(x, y) = 0.




                                             6
                                                 P



                                    grad(g)           grad(f)




                                                                          g(x,y)=0


However g(x, y) = 0 is not a level curve for f (x, y). Thus grad(f ) will most likely not
point perpendicular to g(x, y) = 0. Since grad(f ) points in the direction of maximum
increase of f (x, y) it will be possible to increase f (x, y) by sliding a little around the level
curve in the direction suggested by grad(f ).

If the point P is sitting at a maximum value of f (x, y) then there will be no component
of grad(f ) in the tangential direction through which f (x, y) could be increased. In other
words if P is positioned so that f (x, y) is maximised (or minimised),then grad(f ) will
also point perpendicular to g(x, y) = 0. This means that grad(f ) and grad(g) are parallel
vectors and hence that grad(f ) = ╬╗grad(g). This means that

                                  Ōłéf    Ōłéf       Ōłég   Ōłég
                                     i+    j = ╬╗( i +    j)
                                  Ōłéx    Ōłéy       Ōłéx   Ōłéy
and so
                                   Ōłéf   Ōłég          Ōłéf    Ōłég
                                      =╬╗ ,             =╬╗
                                   Ōłéx   Ōłéx          Ōłéy    Ōłéy
as required.
